summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2009 performance, reporting a 110% increase in profits to $18,525 on $31,156 in sales. The defining event was launching a web version of his desktop application, which doubled conversion rates and reduced support costs. He details successes including price increases, building the A/Bingo testing framework, email marketing via Mailchimp, and a network of holiday bingo mini-sites. He also candidly recounts failures including a $3,500 CSS bug, wasted development on unused desktop features, and ongoing AdWords frustrations. The post closes with his plan to quit his day job in 2010 and go full-time on the business.
key_points: Launching the web version of Bingo Card Creator doubled conversion rates (from 1.17% desktop to 2.33% online) and was the single biggest driver of a 110% profit increase
Email marketing through Mailchimp proved extraordinarily cost-effective at 2 cents per re-engagement versus 24 cents per new trial through AdWords
Building features without talking to users first resulted in three desktop features that only twenty people ever touched — a textbook Lean Startup cautionary tale
Systematizing mini-site creation with freelancers and automation produced greater than 10x ROI on holiday bingo properties
After three years of gradually building toward it, McKenzie has a specific date circled to quit his day job and go full-time on his software business
money_quotes: I'm currently making more than enough money to live off of on Bingo Card Creator (I live rather simply in a rural prefecture in Japan — think Kansas with rather less white people)

It costs me about 24 cents to get a trial download or trial signup through AdWords at the margin, but only 2 cents to remind someone that their account still exists and that it would be perfect for the Halloween Thanksgiving Christmas festivities around the corner.

I'm too cool to actually ask users about their feelings beforehand, of course. Fans of the Lean Startup can already predict how this story ends up, right? To date, twenty users have touched those three features.
Let's see: add marginal feature to application, make twenty users happy, or for just a few hours more develop and market a new application which doubles my revenue streams. Decisions decisions!

In 2007, I had vague dreams of someday going full time on this. In 2008, I had a half-formed wish that maybe I would go full-time on this in 2008. In 2009… I have a date circled in red on my calendar, forms from the tax office and immigration authority ready for submission as soon as the new year starts.
The great thing about it is the whole thing fits in a laptop bag, so aside from some irksome tax and visa issues I have a lot of flexibility in where I live.
Did I mention I lost $3.5k to a CSS bug?
key_insight: A small software business can reach life-changing profitability through relentless conversion optimization, strategic use of web apps over desktop, and cheap re-engagement channels like email — but only if you talk to your users before building features.
